quickId: Crushed workers smell of rotten coconut
habitatNotes: No structural damage. Contaminates exposed food and forms persistent indoor trails, especially after rain pushes colonies inside.
management: Sanitation, moisture correction, and slow-acting sugar baits. Avoid repellent sprays that fragment colonies.

quickId: Single petiole node, evenly rounded thorax
Workers vary widely in size within a colony
habitatNotes: Excavates galleries in moisture-damaged wood. Damage is structural over years, not days; finding frass is the typical first sign.
management: Locate and correct moisture sources. Trace foraging trails back to satellite nests; treat parent colony directly.

quickId: Parallel grooves on head and thorax
Soil mounds along sidewalks and slab edges
habitatNotes: Nuisance only. Soil displacement under slabs occasionally noticeable.
management: Granular or gel baits placed near nest entrances. Seal slab cracks to limit indoor entry.

quickId: Yellow to honey-amber body, darker gaster
Two petiole nodes, 12-segmented antennae with three-segmented club
Multiple satellite nests within heated structures
habitatNotes: Critical concern in hospitals and food-handling facilities. Sprays cause budding and worsen infestations.
management: Bait-only programs across the entire structure. Never use repellent contact insecticides.
